1: The wish — mi yittencha ke'ach li (who will give you to me as a brother) — uses the idiom mi yitten (if only! would that!) characteristic of Hebrew wishes for the impossible or deeply desired (cf. Job 14:13: 'Oh that you would hide me in Sheol'; Jer 9:1: 'Oh that my head were waters'). The underlying social constraint the wish expresses: in the ancient Near East, brothers and sisters could display physical affection publicly (the kiss of greeting was socially sanctioned within the family), but romantic couples showing public affection invited censure. Deuteronomy 22:28-29 and related texts indicate that unmarried couples meeting privately faced social and legal scrutiny. The wish to be treated as a brother is a wish for the social freedom of kinship affection within a romantic relationship — the longing for a covenant that would make what is now constrained socially legitimate and free.
2: The mother's house (beit immi, H517, my mother's house) appears at 3:4 also as the legitimate social container for love — the beloved would bring the bridegroom there, suggesting that the mother's household was the proper venue for courtship within social convention. The mother as the one who teaches (telammedeni, who would instruct/teach me) is the female wisdom figure of the household — in Proverbs, the mother's teaching is explicitly part of the formation of the young (Prov 1:8: 'hear your father's instruction and do not forsake your mother's teaching'). The spiced wine and pomegranate juice (mine-sheqari min-asis rimmonei) are the luxuries of abundance in the Song's vocabulary of sensory pleasure — wine spiced with aromatics and pressed pomegranate juice were elite beverages in the ancient Near East, the products of craft-level processing of agricultural goods.
3: The left-hand/right-hand embrace — semalo tachat roshi ve-yamino techabbakeni (his left hand under my head and his right hand embraces me) — repeats 2:6 verbatim. In ancient Near Eastern love poetry, the reclining embrace with one hand as a pillow was a recognized posture of intimacy; Egyptian tomb paintings and iconography show similar embrace postures. The right hand as the arm of action and the left as the support: in ancient Israelite culture, the right hand was the dominant hand for action, protection, and blessing (Gen 48:13-14; Ps 110:1), while the left hand's supportive role under the head creates the image of complete envelopment. The verbatim repetition of 2:6 here is an example of the Song's structural use of refrain-like repetition across the book's arc.
4: The daughters of Jerusalem adjuration (hishba'ti etkem benot yerushalayim, I adjure you O daughters of Jerusalem) appears for the fourth and final time (2:7, 3:5, 8:4). The earlier two instances invoked 'by the gazelles or the does of the field' — here the animal oath formula is absent, which may signal the maturation of the relationship: the love that needed the protective adjuration is now so firmly established that the oath can stand without the wilderness-animal invocation. The daughters of Jerusalem are the urban audience, the social chorus of the Song who serve as foils for the rural beloved. The content of the adjuration — mah-ta'iru umah-te'oreru et-ha'ahavah ad shetech'pats (why do you stir up or awaken love until it pleases) — consistently warns against forced or premature love. The refrain functions as a wisdom warning embedded in the love poem: love must arise in its own time.
5: The wilderness-procession image (mi zo't olah min-hamidbar mitrapeket al-dodah, who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved?) repeats the form of 3:6's rhetorical question, but now the figure is identified by her relationship: she is leaning on her beloved, whereas 3:6 was an unidentified pillar of smoke. The apple tree (tappuach, H8598) where the mother aroused her beloved and gave birth — the apple or quince tree appears as a sensory pleasure in 2:3 (sitting under his shadow with great delight) and 2:5 (sustain me with apples). The apple tree as the site of both erotic arousal and birth creates a complex of fertility and love imagery. The wilderness-to-apple-tree movement traces the journey from undomesticated desire to rooted, fruitful intimacy: the beloved comes up from the wilderness and arrives at the cultivated garden tree where the covenant of love was first rooted.
6: The seal passage (8:6-7) is the theological and poetic climax of the Song. Seals in the ancient Near East were the primary instruments of personal identity and legal authority: cylinder seals (worn around the neck on a cord) or stamp seals (worn as a ring or pendant on the arm) were used to authenticate documents and ownership. To be a seal on someone's heart or arm was to be their most intimate possession and primary identification mark — Jeremiah 22:24 uses the signet ring on the right hand as an image of intimacy with God (though Coniah's signet would be removed). The comparison of love to death (ki azza kamavet ahavah, for love is strong as death) and jealousy to Sheol (qasha kishol qina'ah, jealousy/passion is fierce as Sheol) uses the two most powerful and inescapable forces in human experience as comparators. Death and Sheol could not be resisted in the ancient Near Eastern worldview; to say love is as strong as these is to say it is as absolute and inescapable. Shalhevetyah — the very flame of the LORD — is a rare compound embedding the divine name (YH = abbreviated YHWH) into the word for flame: the flame of divine love burning in the relationship.
7: The flood-water imagery (mayim rabbim lo yukelu lechabbot et-ha'ahavah, many waters are not able to quench the love; neharot, rivers, cannot drown it) draws on the ANE mythological tradition of the primordial chaos waters as the most threatening force against life and order. In Babylonian cosmology, the primordial Tiamat (chaos-water) was the enemy of the ordered cosmos; in Canaanite mythology, Yam (sea) was the rival of Baal for cosmic dominance. The prophetic literature uses the flood imagery for overwhelming forces of devastation (Isa 8:7-8: the Assyrian flood; Ps 93:3-4: the floods lifting their voices against YHWH). Love that the flood-waters cannot quench is love that withstands the maximum of cosmic threat. The social dimension of v7b — even if a man offered all his house's wealth for love, he would be utterly despised (buz yevuzu lo) — asserts love's non-commercial nature: love cannot be purchased, and the attempt to purchase it invites contempt. Wealth and love exist in incommensurable categories.
8: The brothers' council scene (8:8-9) introduces the brothers as guardians of their sister's honor — a social institution attested throughout the ancient Near East and biblical narrative. In ancient Israelite law and custom, brothers were the primary protectors of unmarried sisters' sexual honor: Genesis 34 (Dinah's brothers avenging her violation); 2 Samuel 13 (Absalom avenging Tamar); Leviticus 21:3 (the priest may defile himself for an unmarried virgin sister). The little sister (achot lanu qetannah, we have a little sister) who has no breasts yet — a younger sibling not yet at marriageable age — represents the brothers' future social responsibility: they must determine how to prepare for the day when she will be sought in marriage (be-yom sheyedubar bah, in the day she is spoken of/negotiated for). The question 'what shall we do for our sister?' reflects the genuine social deliberation about how to protect and honorably present a younger sibling when the time comes.
9: The wall/door alternatives present two possible characters for the younger sister: chomah (H2346, wall) as the woman who maintains firm sexual boundaries, and delet (H1817, door) as the woman who is permissive or easily opened. If she is a wall — maintaining honor — the brothers will build upon her a tirat kesef (H2918 + H3701, a turret/battlement of silver): they will add to and honor her fortifications, adorning her honor. If she is a door — sexually permissive — they will enclose her with boards of cedar (natsur aleha luach erez), sealing her off to prevent further access. The cedar board-enclosure represents not punishment but protection: cedar was the most prestigious building material (the temple's cedar paneling, 1 Kings 6:15-16), so enclosing a door with cedar turns it into a wall of the most valuable material. Both responses, wall and door, end in the brothers' protective action, not condemnation.
10: The beloved's self-identification — ani chomah u-shadai kamigdalot (I am a wall and my breasts are like towers) — picks up the brothers' wall/door binary and answers with the wall: she has been the wall throughout. Az hayiti be'einav kemotse'et shalom (thus I became in his eyes as one who finds peace/favor): the word shalom here plays against the name Solomon (shlomoh), creating a word-level pun where the woman's wholeness/peace is the answer to the king's own name. Shalom as the state of wholeness, well-being, and covenant completion is the beloved's self-description after maintaining integrity: she did not yield prematurely, and in finding peace in the bridegroom's eyes she has achieved the wholeness that the Song has been moving toward throughout. In the allegorical reading, Israel's covenant faithfulness to YHWH produces the shalom that is the covenant's promise (Num 6:26).
11: Solomon's vineyard at Ba'al Hamon (H1174, lord of abundance or lord of the multitude) is an unidentified location — no archaeological site has been securely identified with this name, which suggests either a legendary place or a poetic toponym meaning 'place of great abundance.' The vineyard-keeper arrangement (natan et-ha-kerem la-notreim, he gave the vineyard to the keepers) for a thousand pieces of silver each reflects actual ancient Near Eastern viticulture management: large vineyards were commonly managed through tenant-keeper arrangements where the owner received a fixed portion of the harvest value. Isaiah 7:23 uses the thousand-silver-vine as an image of the most valuable possible vineyard. The economic framework of vineyard value — royal vineyard worth thousands of silver per keeper — sets up the beloved's contrast in v12.
12: The climax of the vineyard imagery (karmi shelli lefanai, my own vineyard is before me, mine) — the emphatic shelli (mine, my own, from the preposition shel + first person) resolves the vine-narrative that opened the Song. In 1:6, the beloved said 'my own vineyard I have not kept' — she was forced to tend her brothers' vineyards at the expense of her own. Now at the Song's close she asserts full possession: her vineyard is completely her own and is before her (intact, unharvested, fully hers to offer). The thousand to Solomon and the two hundred to the keepers continues the economic framework of v11 but with a different referent: now the beloved herself distributes the vineyard's value according to her own sovereignty, with the two hundred going to 'those who keep the fruit.' In the allegorical reading, Israel's vineyard is YHWH's covenant relationship; the beloved keeping her own vineyard represents covenant faithfulness returned to its proper owner.
13: The bridegroom's call to the garden-dwelling beloved — hayyoshevet ba-gannim chaverim maqshivim le-qolech hashme'ini (O you who dwell in the gardens, the companions are listening for your voice — let me hear it) — introduces the community of companions (chaverim, H2270, friends/companions) who are also listening for the beloved's voice. This communal dimension — that the beloved's speech is sought not only by the bridegroom but by an audience of companions — mirrors the Song's own social performance context: the poem was sung in community. In Jewish tradition, the Song was sung at weddings and festival celebrations; the companions listening for the beloved's voice are the wedding guests, the community witnessing the love relationship. The beloved who dwells in gardens (yoshevet ba-gannim) has achieved the garden-paradise that was the implicit goal of the Song's pastoral imagery throughout.
14: The Song's final verse — berach dodi vedameh lekha litsvi o le'ofer ha'ayyalim al-harei vesamim (flee, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices) — returns to the gazelle/stag image of 2:9, 2:17, and 4:6, completing the Song's circular structure. The verb berach (flee, H1272) is the same verb used in 2:17 and 4:6 for the stag's departure at dawn — suggesting not a permanent farewell but the movement that precedes a return: the beloved sends the bridegroom out into the mountains of spices (harei vesamim), the fragrant landscape where he will be most himself, with the implicit invitation to return. The Song ends not with consummation described but with anticipation extended — a structural feature consistent with the Song's overall movement through longing, encounter, separation, and renewed longing. This ending is eschatologically charged: the love celebrated throughout the Song is not yet fully realized; its completion lies ahead, in the same direction as the stag bounding over the mountains.
